pattern: Because using one extra subarray never decreases the best achievable total by more than the one before it, the best-value-for-count curve is concave, so paying a per-subarray penalty and letting a sliding-window DP pick freely finds the best trade-off without ever tracking the count directly.
hints: For a fixed 'price' charged per subarray used, the best total (value minus price times count) can be computed with a simple sliding-window DP over subarray lengths in [l, r], with no need to track how many subarrays were used.

As that per-subarray price rises from zero, the number of subarrays the unconstrained DP prefers to use only ever goes down -- binary search on the price for the smallest one that brings the preferred count to at most m.

Once the right price is found, the answer is the DP's best score at that price plus price times m directly (not price times whatever count the DP happened to report), which correctly handles ties where several counts are equally good; if even one subarray is never worth using, fall back to the single best length-[l,r] window.

edge_inputs.description: two subarrays of different lengths within range combine for the best total
the cap of subarrays is used fully with lengths at the small end of the range
only one subarray is allowed even though more would fit
every value is negative, so the least negative single subarray is still forced by the at-least-one rule
l equals r, forcing every subarray to the same fixed length
m is larger than the number of subarrays that can possibly fit, so the cap is never binding
a tied choice between using one long subarray or two shorter ones covering similar territory

stress.description: random values and ranges, moderate size so the brute oracle can cross-check quickly
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 130))
    nums = [rng.randint(-1000, 1000) for _ in range(n)]
    l = rng.randint(1, n)
    r = rng.randint(l, n)
    m = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [nums, m, l, r]

stress.description: many negative values with a narrow length window to stress the at-least-one fallback
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 130))
    nums = [rng.randint(-1000000000, -1) for _ in range(n)]
    l = rng.randint(1, min(5, n))
    r = rng.randint(l, min(l + 3, n))
    m = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [nums, m, l, r]
